name: Big Shoulders
classification_notes: A narrow, condensed American Gothic family with strong vertical stress and upright letterforms, characteristic of the gothic grotesque tradition used in American signage and civic design.
summary: A narrow American Gothic family commissioned for the Chicago Design System, offered free across three stylistic variants and two optical sizes.
description: Designed for the Chicago Design System and available free via Google Fonts, the family spans Big Shoulders, Big Shoulders Inline and Big Shoulders Stencil, each offered in Text and Display optical cuts. Nine weights from Thin to Black are available across all three variants, with variable font support throughout. The stencil and inline cuts extend its utility into signage, wayfinding and civic applications where decorative weight is appropriate.
foundry_blurb: Big Shoulders is described as a narrow American Gothic type family, originally designed for the Chicago Design System and made freely available to all. The family encompasses Big Shoulders, Big Shoulders Stencil and Big Shoulders Inline, each available in Text and Display optical sizes across a full weight range.

name: Citrine
classification_notes: Citrine draws from typewriter and early PC screen typography traditions, with a large x-height, round joints and flat-sided curved forms typical of humanist sans-serif design optimised for readability.
summary: A screen-optimised humanist sans loosely derived from the Epoca typewriter typeface, built for writing and document work across five weights with true small caps.
description: Rooted in the Epoca typeface designed for the Hermes 3000 typewriter, Citrine takes that model's essential widths and spacing and reworks them for modern screen reading with flat-sided curves, a double-barrel lowercase a and tighter tracking. A large x-height and round joints keep it approachable at small sizes, while true small caps sized to the lowercase add typographic range. Available as a variable font spanning five named weights from Light to Bold.
foundry_blurb: XO Type Co describe Citrine as drawing upon machine-driven typography from the first American PC boom of the 1980s, referencing word processing typefaces made for easy reading at low resolution and in-game typography. Loosely based on Epoca, it adds flat-sided curved forms, a double-barrel lowercase a and tighter spacing for modern screen use, and includes true small caps sized to the lowercase for each of its five weights.

name: Bronzo
classification_notes: Bronzo is a display typeface with strong geometric construction rooted in Modernist ideals, drawing on Russian Constructivist forms, Geometric Art Deco and American Streamline styling. Its distinctive asymmetric stroke logic and uncomfortably wide proportions place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A revival of Rick Valicenti and Mouli Marur's 1989 geometric display typeface, expanded from three weights to nine with new language support and redesigned accents.
description: Originally drawn by hand at Thirst in Chicago to accompany Gilbert paper's Esse line, Bronzo became iconic enough to launch the Thirstype foundry in 1990 before disappearing when that studio closed. This XO Type Co redesign reconstructs its core tension: asymmetric centre strokes, incomplete curves that always break right, and proportions that sit uneasily between square and rectangle. The result evokes Russian Constructivism, Geometric Art Deco and American Streamline simultaneously, and is explicitly designed to be stretched, warped and slanted.
foundry_blurb: XO Type Co describe Bronzo as a distillation of durable ideas spanning 150 years of type design, with references to Russian Constructivist forcefulness, Geometric Art Deco grace and American Streamline exuberance. Originally created at Thirst by Rick Valicenti and Mouli Marur in 1989, this redesign expands the family from three hand-drawn weights to nine, adds new language support and totally redesigned accents, while preserving the typeface's intrinsic tension and forward momentum.

name: Gia
classification_notes: Gia draws on the sinewy, curved unicase letterforms of 1960s–1980s digital tech branding and arcade culture, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric, retro-futurist character.
summary: A seven-weight display family with unicase options and small caps, drawn from the curved letterforms of 1960s–1980s tech and arcade culture.
description: Named after model Gia Carangi, this family captures the sinewy, rounded letterforms that defined early digital tech branding from NASA to Atari and Sega. Seven weights run from Ultralite to Black, with a variable font available, and the family includes unicase options and true small caps throughout. Well suited to titles, logotypes and screen work where a retro-futurist tone is needed.
foundry_blurb: XO Type Co describe Gia as a family designed after the iconic letterforms of the first digital tech revolution of the 1960s to 1980s, citing references including NASA, The Buggles, Atari, Devo and Apple. Available in seven weights with a variable font, unicase options and true small caps, and named after Gia Carangi, the iconic face of early 1980s pop fashion.

name: Havelock Titling
classification_notes: A geometric display sans built around the reduction of letterforms to square, triangle and circle, with rounded joints for aerodynamic fluidity. Classified as display due to its titling-specific application and highly constructed geometric character.
summary: A titling-weight geometric sans by Patric King that reduces each letterform to its most elemental shapes, with rounded joints to counter any rigidity.
description: Built on the same underlying geometry as the layerable Havelock family, Havelock Titling strips away the inline, stencil and multiline variants to deliver a standalone typeface across five weights from Light to Black, also available as a variable font. Letterforms are reduced to square, triangle and circle with rounded joints that give each weight an aerodynamic quality while avoiding the stiffness that pure geometry can produce. Suited to headlines and identities where clean, highly constructed forms are wanted without the layering apparatus of the parent family.
foundry_blurb: XO Type Co describe Havelock Titling's essential idea as 'pure geometry with austere spacing', with each letter reduced to only its required forms and joints rounded to give an aerodynamic sense of motion. The typeface grew out of the broader Havelock family after designers responded to its geometry but wanted to use it without the layering effects, leading to a dedicated titling version available in five weights and as a variable font.

name: Havelock
classification_notes: An all-caps display family built explicitly for layering and transparency effects, with four stylistic variants (Solid, Inline, Stencil, Multiline) designed to stack on top of one another. The geometric letterforms and graphic nature place it firmly in the display category.
summary: An all-caps display family of four variants — Solid, Inline, Stencil and Multiline — engineered to be stacked in layers with transparency for highly graphic typographic compositions.
description: Each of the four styles is designed with cutouts calibrated to nest within one another, so any combination of layers produces a coherent visual result regardless of stacking order. The geometry reads as almost neutral at a glance, but the letterforms carry very specific rhythm and spatial character that becomes apparent on close inspection. A companion family, Havelock Titling, extracts the core shapes into five weights from Light to Black for contexts where the layering system is not required.
foundry_blurb: XO Type Co describe Havelock as four related typefaces built to layer with transparency, combining hard and soft qualities, geometry and pattern. Solid acts as the base layer, with Inline, Stencil and Multiline sitting above it — each with cutouts drawn to fit neatly within each other in any combination. They note that blending with translucent colour can produce particularly striking results. Havelock Titling emerged afterwards, offering the family's essential geometric shapes across five weights for use without layering effects.

name: Royal Street
classification_notes: A condensed grotesque sans-serif, explicitly described as such by the foundry, designed for all-caps headline use in the tradition of tabloid condensed grotesques.
summary: A six-weight condensed grotesque built for headlines, drawing on the all-caps tabloid tradition with an internet-era sensibility.
description: Six weights of condensed grotesque running from Extra Light to Black, all designed around the tabloid convention of the condensed all-caps headline. Each weight includes true small caps, alternate upper and lowercase forms, case-sensitive punctuation and ligatures, with support for 87 languages. The tone is deliberately American and hyperbolic, lending itself to editorial display, campaign headlines and expressive typesetting at large sizes.
foundry_blurb: XO Type Co describe Royal Street as a sleek, modern collection of six condensed fonts for headlines, rooted in American pop and hyperbole. They frame it as an Internet-Era take on the condensed all-caps tabloid headline, offering true small caps, alternate shapes, case-sensitive punctuation, ligatures and support for 87 languages.

name: Rocinante Titling
classification_notes: A tall, narrow titling face with geometric influences and deliberately enlarged lightwells; the condensed proportions and high stroke contrast place it firmly in the display category rather than text use.
summary: A tall, narrow display typeface built around deliberately oversized lightwells that turn a structural necessity into a defining visual detail.
description: The family is organised around the tension between interior and exterior space: lightwells are enlarged until they read as a deliberate design choice rather than a technical workaround, creating a distinctive squareness at joints and intersections. Five weights from Light to Black each carry an oblique companion, and the family ships as a variable font for continuous weight interpolation. Stroke weights and spacing are matched precisely to those of Havelock Titling, allowing the two families to be set together while preserving rhythmic consistency.
foundry_blurb: XO Type Co describe Rocinante Titling as a study in interior and exterior tension within a tall, somewhat geometric frame, with lightwells expanded and enlarged to function as a declared design detail rather than a functional fix. They note that its stroke weights and spacing align exactly with those of Havelock Titling, enabling combinations between the two families that trade the narrowness and tense squareness of Rocinante against the luxurious roundness of Havelock.

name: Okoye
classification_notes: Okoye sits between 19th-century grotesque curviness and 20th-century neutral sans, with monolinear light weights and high contrast in heavy weights — a contemporary grotesque with deliberate quirks.
summary: A nine-weight grotesque that transitions from near-monolinear in its light cuts to high-contrast in its heaviest, with handwritten traces in bowl construction and angular stylistic alternates.
description: Bowls sweep into stems on b, d, p and q in a manner that recalls handwriting, while small jutting details on the g and Q introduce angular counterpoints. Stylistic alternates (SS01) extend that energy to K, R, g and k, and spacing tightens progressively from open and airy in the lightest weights to close and imposing at Black. Small caps mirror full-size caps exactly, and the family ships with arrows and extensive Latin language coverage, making it usable across long-form text, labels and interface work.
foundry_blurb: XO Type Co describe Okoye as occupying a liminal space between the curviness of 19th-century grotesques and the sandblasted neutrality of 20th-century models, aiming for neutrality with character retained. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black with full variable font support, extensive Latin language coverage, small caps and arrows, and is built to align with CSS font-weight values 100–900 out of the box.
